architecture_summary: The paper proposes Deformable Attention Transformer (DAT), a hierarchical Vision Transformer backbone that replaces vanilla self-attention with a deformable self-attention module. DAT learns query-agnostic offsets to shift keys and values toward important regions, and uses local attention in early stages plus deformable attention in later stages for image classification and dense prediction tasks.

novel_contributions: a novel deformable self-attention module where the positions of key and value pairs in self-attention are selected in a data-dependent way
Deformable Attention Transformer (DAT), a general backbone model with deformable attention for both image classification and dense prediction tasks
the first deformable self-attention backbone for visual recognition
a simple and efficient deformable self-attention design with linear space complexity
query agnostic offsets to shift keys and values to important regions

key_claims.claim: The paper proposes a novel deformable self-attention module that selects key and value positions in a data-dependent way, enabling the model to focus on relevant regions and capture more informative features.
DAT is presented as a general backbone model for both image classification and dense prediction tasks.

The authors claim their model outperforms competitive baselines including Swin Transformer consistently, by 0.7 on top-1 accuracy, 1.2 on mIoU, and 1.1 on object detection for both box AP and mask AP.
DAT uses query agnostic offsets and shared deformed keys and values to achieve an efficient trade-off with linear space complexity.
On ImageNet-1K classification, DAT outperforms Swin Transformer counterparts by +0.7, +0.7 and +0.5 across the three scales, and DAT-B improves further to 84.8 at 384×384.
On COCO object detection and instance segmentation, DAT achieves consistent improvements over Swin Transformer in RetinaNet, Mask R-CNN, and Cascade Mask R-CNN settings.
On ADE20K semantic segmentation, DAT improves over Swin Transformer at each of three model scales.

The ablation studies show that deformable relative position bias is more compatible with deformable attention than fixed learnable position bias or depth-wise convolution, and that using deformable attention in later stages is more effective than in early stages.
